question: Explain, in simple terms, the core assumption that Naive Bayes makes about the features in a dataset and why this assumption is considered 'naive'.
expectedPoints: Assumes that all features are conditionally independent of each other given the class label.
This simplifies the computation of the joint probability P(features|class) to the product of individual probabilities P(feature_i|class).
It is considered 'naive' because this assumption of complete independence is very rarely true in real-world data, yet the method often performs well despite this simplification.

question: Describe the core difference between discriminative and generative models in machine learning. Provide one example of a probability-based algorithm for each type.
expectedPoints: Discriminative models learn the boundary between classes; they model the conditional probability P(Class | Features) directly.
Generative models learn the distribution of the data for each class; they model the joint probability P(Features, Class) and use Bayes' Theorem to infer P(Class | Features).
Example of discriminative: Logistic Regression. Example of generative: Naive Bayes Classifier.

question: What is the primary goal of the Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation, and how does it differ from Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)?
expectedPoints: The goal of MAP estimation is to find the mode of the posterior distribution, i.e., the most probable hypothesis given the observed data and a prior belief.
It incorporates prior knowledge or belief about the parameters (the prior probability) into the estimation process.
MLE, in contrast, only seeks to find the parameter values that maximize the likelihood of the observed data, P(Data | Parameters), with no consideration for prior beliefs.
